question: Distinguish precisely among Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts. Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer, which has the highest count, and why?
facts: A Service Line is defined as an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service.
The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons.

A Starlink Subscriber is defined as a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team.
Starlink Subscriber is a subset of Service Lines.
Starlink Subscribers excludes managed enterprise and government customers with negotiated contracts.
Starlink Subscribers are calculated for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period that meet the non-negotiated-account criterion.
Starlink Subscribers were approximately 10.3 million as of March 31, 2026.
Supported accounts is defined as a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok.
The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active.
Supported accounts were over 1.3 billion active in the LTM ended March 31, 2026.

Service Line is the most analogous to a revenue-generating customer because it is explicitly billed according to Starlink's service plans and is the most inclusive revenue-generating metric on the Starlink side.
Supported accounts has the highest count at over 1.3 billion.

Supported accounts has the highest count because it captures any account that simply logged into X or Grok during the trailing twelve months, expressly permits fake, spam, and bot accounts to be counted, requires no subscription payment or physical terminal deployment, and spans two large consumer platforms.

A 5-for-1 split multiplies the float fivefold.

Underwriters generally prefer an offering price within a range that is familiar to the market and compatible with exchange listing standards.

Certain index inclusion methodologies and exchange listing criteria reference per-share price thresholds.

question: When evaluating future orbital AI compute satellites, which disclosed accounting policies or asset categories provide the closest precedent for treatment? Does the filing explicitly resolve whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed?
facts: The filing does not explicitly resolve whether future orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed.
Existing satellites are classified as Property, plant, and equipment (PP&E), stated at cost less accumulated depreciation.
Capitalized costs for existing satellites include build costs plus capitalized launch costs.
Useful lives are 5 years for broadband satellites and 3 years for first-generation mobile satellites.
Depreciation for existing satellites is straight-line over estimated useful lives.
A one-year change in average satellite useful life would have moved 2025 operating income by approximately $480 million.
AI infrastructure and data centers are classified as PP&E.
Useful lives for servers and networking equipment are 5–6 years.
Useful lives for data center infrastructure are 20–25 years.
Interest is capitalized during construction for significant long-term projects such as AI infrastructure data centers.
Construction in Progress (CIP) is primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of facilities and equipment as well as AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service.
Because Starship is in the development stage, the majority of costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred.

Once Starship reaches commercialization, costs generally will be capitalized rather than expensed as R&D, and depreciated into the cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered.
Maintenance and repairs are expensed as incurred.
Refurbishment and enhancements that extend life or functionality are capitalized and depreciated ratably.
By inference from disclosed precedents, development-stage costs for orbital AI compute satellites would likely be expensed to R&D.
By inference from disclosed precedents, post-deployment operational assets for orbital AI compute satellites would likely be capitalized as PP&E.

question: The filing discloses two distinct voting mechanisms: (i) Class B shareholders voting as a class to elect a majority of directors, and (ii) combined Class A and Class B voting power determining the remaining board seats and general shareholder matters. Explain precisely how this dual-mechanism structure differs economically and practically from a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure in which all directors are elected by the combined class vote. What is the specific implication for a Class A shareholder's ability to influence board composition even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of economic interest?
facts: In a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure, Class B's control of the board is contingent and proportional to its economic stake.
In a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure, Class A can mathematically overcome Class B's 10× multiplier if Class A accumulates more than ~90.9% of the economic interest.
In SpaceX's structure, Class B voting separately as a class elects 51% of the total authorized directors for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding.
In SpaceX's structure, Class A and Class B voting together as a single class elect the remaining 49% of directors (the Common Stock Directors) and decide all general shareholder matters.
In SpaceX's structure, the 51% Class B Director block is not a function of relative economic ownership.
In SpaceX's structure, so long as a single Class B share remains outstanding, Class B elects 51% of the board regardless of whether Class A holds 10%, 50%, 90%, or 99.99% of the economic interest.
In SpaceX's structure, there is no economic threshold Class A can cross to capture the Class B Director seats.
In SpaceX's structure, the 51% Class B Director block is immune to dilution from equity issuances of Class A or Class C.
In SpaceX's structure, Class B Directors may be removed only by a majority of Class B voting separately as a class.
In SpaceX's structure, vacancies in Class B seats may be filled only by Class B or the remaining Class B Directors, and not any other persons.
In SpaceX's structure, Class A's maximum achievable board representation is the Common Stock Directors (49% of the board, rounded down).
In SpaceX's structure, a Class A economic majority cannot translate into board control.
In SpaceX's structure, no amount of Class A accumulation changes the 51% Class B block.
In SpaceX's structure, Class A cannot remove a Class B Director even with unanimous Class A support.
In SpaceX's structure, Class B participates with 10× weighting in the combined-class vote to remove Common Stock Directors.
In SpaceX's structure, a Class A shareholder with a majority of economic interest is a permanent minority in board-composition terms.
SpaceX's structure converts a proportional, dilutable, threshold-dependent founder lock into an absolute, dilution-proof, threshold-free lock on a board majority.
For a Class A shareholder, holding a majority of the economic interest is economically real but governance-irrelevant with respect to the majority of the board.
